content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Play it here: https://aaronphilip2003.github.io/rust-pong/
There’s a rite of passage in game development, and it isn’t building the next indie darling: it’s building Pong. Everyone knows the rules. Nobody argues about scope. And yet, the moment you try to actually ship it, not just run it on your laptop, but put it on the web, for anyone to click and play, Pong becomes a surprisingly good teacher. Mine taught me more about WebAssembly, linkers, and browser security policies than I expected from a game where the hardest math is “did the ball hit the paddle.”
Here’s how a single-player Pong clone, written entirely in Rust, went from cargo new to a live, playable game at a github.io URL, and every wall I ran into along the way.
Why Rust, and why Macroquad
The brief was simple: a polished single-player Pong clone, playable against a computer opponent, that could run natively during development and compile to WebAssembly for deployment. No Bevy, no ggez, just something lightweight enough to stay beginner-friendly while still producing a real, professional project structure.
That pointed straight at Macroquad, a minimal Rust game framework with almost no external dependencies. Macroquad’s biggest selling point for this project wasn’t its rendering API, it was the fact that the exact same source code compiles natively with cargo run and to wasm32-unknown-unknown with one extra flag. No platform-specific branches, no conditional compilation. Write the game once.
Keeping the architecture boring (in a good way)
Pong doesn’t need a fancy architecture, but it deserves a clean one. The project split into small, single-responsibility modules:

• constants.rs: every tunable number in the game (paddle speed, ball speed, AI reaction speed, winning score) lives here, so balancing the game later is a one-line edit, not a hunt through gameplay code.

• paddle.rs: a Paddle struct shared by both the human player and the computer, handling movement, screen clamping, and drawing.

• ball.rs: motion, wall bounces, and the gradual speed-up after every paddle hit (capped, so rallies never become physically unfair).

• ai.rs: a Difficulty enum (Easy / Medium / Hard) and a deliberately simple rules-based tracking function. No machine learning here, the "AI" just chases the ball's vertical position at a capped speed. Difficulty is entirely a function of how fast that cap is.

• game.rs: the state machine (Menu → Playing → GameOver), scoring, and the trickiest part of the whole project: collision detection.

• main.rs: a thin entry point that just sets up the window and drives the game loop.
That last point about collision detection is worth dwelling on. AABB collision is easy to get approximately right and surprisingly easy to get subtly wrong. The two failure modes I was most careful to avoid:

• Ball clipping through a paddle at high speed, because the collision check only looked at the current frame’s position instead of resolving overlap properly.

• Double-triggering a single hit, the ball rectangle can still overlap the paddle for a frame or two after bouncing, and a naive check will flip its velocity back and forth like a strobe light.
The fix for both was to only register a bounce when the ball’s velocity was actually moving into the paddle (vel.x < 0.0 for the left paddle, vel.x > 0.0 for the right), and to snap the ball's position to the paddle's edge immediately after a bounce rather than trusting the physics to sort it out next frame.
Compiling for the web: the first surprise
Getting cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown --release to actually produce a working binary was where things got interesting.
The first build failed at the linker stage, not the compiler stage, always a slightly ominous sign. The error was a wall of undefined symbol messages: console_log, init_webgl, canvas_width, glGetIntegerv, and a dozen more. These weren't bugs in the game code. They're functions Macroquad expects the browser's JavaScript to provide at runtime, things like WebGL calls and canvas sizing, and they're never meant to be resolved at compile time.
The catch: newer Rust toolchains changed the default linker behavior for the wasm32-unknown-unknown target. Older Rust versions silently left unresolved external symbols alone, trusting the host environment (the browser) to supply them later. Newer versions treat that as an error unless you explicitly say otherwise. The fix turned out to be a single, easy-to-miss file:
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.wasm32-unknown-unknown]
rustflags = ["-C", "link-args=--allow-undefined"]Three lines, and the entire wall of linker errors disappeared.
Automating the build with GitHub Actions
Rather than manually rebuilding the WASM binary and copying it into a docs/ folder every time I changed the game, I set up a GitHub Actions workflow to do it on every push: install the Rust toolchain, add the wasm32-unknown-unknown target, run the release build, and deploy the result straight to GitHub Pages. Push to main, and a minute or two later the live site updates itself. It's a small thing, but it's the difference between "deploying" feeling like a chore and feeling like it isn't a step at all.
The game that loaded… into a tiny black box
With the linker fixed, the Actions workflow went green, and the site went live. And it showed… a small black rectangle, roughly the size of a browser’s default canvas element, with nothing drawn in it.
This is where debugging a browser-based Rust game stops feeling like Rust debugging at all and starts feeling like web debugging with extra steps.
First red herring: the browser console showed a Content Security Policy error blocking eval(). This looked like a server misconfiguration, except GitHub Pages doesn't set a CSP header by default, and there wasn't one in the page's HTML either. Testing in an incognito window with extensions disabled confirmed it: a browser extension (one of the security/privacy tools installed) was injecting its own CSP into every page, and it happened to block something Macroquad's WebAssembly loader script needed internally. Nothing wrong with the deployment, just a reminder that "it doesn't work" and "it doesn't work for me, in my browser, with my extensions" are very different bug reports.
With the CSP explained, the tiny black box remained. The console was quiet, just a few harmless warnings about optional WebGL2 functions the loader script didn’t implement. No crash, no fatal error. The WASM file and its JavaScript loader both downloaded fine. So why wasn’t anything drawing?
The answer was almost embarrassingly simple: on the web, Macroquad doesn’t size the canvas using the window_conf() dimensions you set in Rust (800×600, in this case). It sizes the canvas based on the actual CSS dimensions of the <canvas> element on the page. The original index.html styled the canvas with display: block; margin: 0 auto;, no explicit width or height, so the browser fell back to its intrinsic default canvas size: 300×150 pixels. The game was running the entire time. It was just rendering into a box the size of a postage stamp.
The fix was CSS, not Rust:
html, body, canvas {
margin: 0;
padding: 0;
width: 100%;
height: 100%;
overflow: hidden;
position: absolute;
background: black;
z-index: 0;
}Stretch the canvas to fill the page, and the game, which was already calling screen_width() and screen_height() rather than hardcoding pixel values, resized itself correctly on the next reload.
What actually shipped
At the end of it, the deployed game has:

• Smooth, frame-rate-independent paddle movement (W/S for the player)

• A rules-based AI opponent with three real difficulty levels

• Proper AABB collision detection with no clipping and no double-hit bugs

• A full menu → playing → game-over state flow, first to 10 points wins

• Pause and a live FPS counter

• One codebase, two build targets: a native desktop binary and a browser-playable WebAssembly build, deployed automatically on every push
The actual lesson
None of the real problems here were about Rust’s borrow checker, or game physics, or even Macroquad’s API. They were about the seams between systems: what the compiler assumes versus what the linker will allow, what your HTML assumes versus what the browser actually renders, what your CSP assumes versus what a well-meaning extension enforces. Writing the game was the fun part. Shipping it was the part that taught me something.
If you’re building something similar, my honest advice is: get it compiling for the web early, not last. The gameplay bugs are the easy ones. The deployment bugs are where you actually learn how the web works.
